elements.sent-2411-001: Graphs inherently capture dependencies between nodes or variables through their topological structure, with paths between any two nodes indicating a sequential dependency on the nodes traversed.
elements.sent-2411-002: Message Passing Neural Networks (MPNNs) leverage these latent relationships embedded in graph structures, and have become widely adopted across diverse applications.
elements.sent-2411-003: However, many existing methods predominantly rely on local information within the $1$-hop neighborhood.
elements.sent-2411-004: This approach has notable limitations; for example, $1$-hop aggregation schemes inherently lose long-distance information, and are limited in expressive power as defined by the $k$-Weisfeiler-Leman ($k$-WL) isomorphism test.
elements.sent-2411-005: To address these issues, we propose the Higher Order Graphical Attention (HoGA) module, which assigns weights to variable-length paths sampled based on feature-vector diversity, effectively reconstructing the $k$-hop neighborhood.
elements.sent-2411-006: HoGA represents higher-order relationships as a robust form of self-attention, applicable to any single-hop attention mechanism.
elements.sent-2411-007: In empirical studies, applying HoGA to existing attention-based models consistently leads to significant accuracy improvements on benchmark node classification datasets.
elements.sent-2411-008: Furthermore, we observe that the performance degradation typically associated with additional message-passing steps may be mitigated.
elements.sent-2411-009: Message Passing Neural Networks (MPNN) \cite{gilmer2017mpnn represent a feature-extraction paradigm that relies on the inherent variable relationships described by the topology of a graph \cite{scarselli2009graph for tasks over non-Euclidean domains.}}
elements.sent-2411-010: MPNNs have found wide-ranging applications across various fields, including molecular graphs in chemistry \cite{gilmer2017mpnn , transport systems planning \cite{jiang2022graph , social networks \cite{fan2019graph , drug discovery \cite{xiong2021graph , and climate modeling \cite{lam2023graphcast .}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-011: Initial work on MPNNs \cite{velickovic2018graph, kipf2016semi, xu2018powerful, gilmer2017mpnn utilized strictly local information contained within the $1$-hop neighborhood of the node of interest.}
elements.sent-2411-012: However, to represent critical long-distance relationships, such as teleconnections between atmospheric events on an embedded world graph \cite{lam2023graphcast , multiple message propagation steps are necessary.}
elements.sent-2411-013: As a consequence, additional layers must be added to the network, each of which compresses the contents of the message.
elements.sent-2411-014: Oversquashing refers to the excessive message compression due to the finite hidden buffer size and high commute time between nodes \cite{di2023over .}
elements.sent-2411-015: Furthermore, due to performance drops, single-hop MPNN layers constrain network depth \cite{wang2020multi , causing the loss of long-distance signals in the $k$-hop neighborhoods.}

elements.sent-2411-016: Multi-hop aggregation schemes aggregate feature-vectors within a specified distance from a node, capturing higher-order dependencies in the graph's topology.
elements.sent-2411-017: These dependencies include the delayed influence of one node's message on another through paths longer than one hop.
elements.sent-2411-018: Walk-based methods consider the topology of paths between nodes for message passing \cite{yeh2023random, li2020distance, michel2023path , sometimes leveraging explicit embeddings \cite{kong2022geodesic .}}
elements.sent-2411-019: Alternatively, methods such as \cite{abboud2022shortest directly aggregate the entire $k$-hop neighborhood into a single feature-vector.}
elements.sent-2411-020: A critical issue in non-walk-oriented methods is the intractability when parameterising the $k$-hop neighborhood, which typically exhibits exponential growth in cardinality.
elements.sent-2411-021: Nevertheless, the $k$-hop neighborhood contains content-rich high-order relationships that already indirectly describe feature-vector evolution given additional message-passing operations.
elements.sent-2411-022: A hash function that takes as input all nodes from each $k$-hop neighbourhood set exhibits strongly desirable theoretical properties \cite{zhang2023rethinking .}
elements.sent-2411-023: This approach offers expressivity significantly greater than the first-order Weisfeiler-Leman ($1$-WL) test and the ability to distinguish non-isomorphic graphs containing cut-vertices.
elements.sent-2411-024: We introduce the Higher Order Graph Attention (HoGA) module, which efficiently parameterises the $k$-hop neighborhood by learning self-attention weights for the shortest paths of length $k$, as illustrated in Figure \ref{fig:front_fig .}
elements.sent-2411-025: Unlike other higher-order aggregation methods \cite{abboud2022shortest , HoGA samples a subset from the power set of the $k$-hop neighborhood using a heuristically-driven probabilistic walk on an augmented graph, where edges exist only if a shortest path of length $k$ connects two nodes in the original graph.}
elements.sent-2411-026: Critically, HoGA prevents smoothing feature-vectors by capturing the modalities within the $k$-hop neighborhood while ensuring that parameterisation scales linearly with the number of edges.
elements.sent-2411-027: Our approach contrasts with existing $k$-order attention techniques that derive similarity scores from fixed topological substructures, such as $k$-simplexes \cite{huang2024higher or $k$-neighborhoods \cite{zhang2024hongat .}}
elements.sent-2411-028: These structures limit expressiveness; learning attention weights for fixed substructures reduces network generalisability, and their intractable growth constrains the network's $k$-order \cite{huang2024higher .}
elements.sent-2411-029: We empirically show that incorporating the Higher Order Attention (HoGA) module into previously state-of-the-art attention models \cite{chamberlain2021grand, velickovic2018graph significantly improves accuracy over benchmark node classification datasets \cite{yang2016revisiting .}}
elements.sent-2411-030: We conduct additional experiments, including an ablation study on the number of layers and an analysis of the effect of the node sampling method on accuracy.
elements.sent-2411-031: Our contributions are summarized as follows.
elements.sent-2411-032: We propose HoGA, a graph attention module that relies on sampling the $k$-hop neighborhood via a heuristic walk.
elements.sent-2411-033: HoGA extends any single-hop method to a $k$-hop setting.
elements.sent-2411-034: We demonstrate the efficacy of HoGA on the GAT and GRAND models.
elements.sent-2411-035: Our higher-order attention module leverages information from an arbitrary distance from the node of interest, thereby substantially mitigating the oversquashing phenomenon.
elements.sent-2411-036: We also show that oversmoothing is reduced, using accuracy as a proxy metric.
elements.sent-2411-037: We perform empirical studies showcasing the benefit of the HoGA module and, in particular, show significantly higher accuracy over key datasets for node classification tasks.
elements.sent-2411-038: Our attention module emerges as a simple and effective way to harness long-distance information.

elements.sent-2411-185: We proposed the Higher Order Graphical Attention (HoGA) module, which extends existing forms of single-hop self-attention methods to a $k$-hop setting.

elements.sent-2411-188: In doing so, we have demonstrated that direct sampling of the $k$-hop neighborhood is a viable competitor to walk-based methods \cite{wang2019heterogeneous, kong2022geodesic, michel2023path and other higher-order methods \cite{abboud2022shortest, huang2024higher, zhang2024hongat .. {\bf Future Directions.}}}
elements.sent-2411-189: Our empirical study indicates that the efficacy of HoGA depends on the sampling method's efficiency in capturing modes of distribution within a limited number of sampling iterations.
elements.sent-2411-190: More efficient, potentially feed-forward methods could enhance performance by improving modality compression, enabling faster convergence or more dynamic behavior in the walk process.
elements.sent-2411-191: Graphs model latent variable relationships in many real-world systems, and Message Passing Neural Networks (MPNNs) are widely used to learn such structures for downstream tasks.
elements.sent-2411-192: While edge-based MPNNs effectively capture local interactions, their expressive power is theoretically bounded, limiting the discovery of higher-order relationships.
elements.sent-2411-193: We introduce the Higher-Order Graph Attention (HoGA) module, which constructs a $k$-order attention matrix by sampling subgraphs to maximize diversity among feature vectors.
elements.sent-2411-194: Unlike existing higher-order attention methods that greedily resample similar $k$-order relationships, HoGA targets diverse modalities in higher-order topology, reducing redundancy and expanding the range of captured substructures.
elements.sent-2411-195: Applied to two single-hop attention models, HoGA achieves at least a 5\% accuracy gain on all benchmark node classification datasets and outperforms recent baselines on six of eight datasets.
elements.sent-2411-197: Message Passing Neural Networks (MPNNs) capture latent relationships relevant to downstream tasks \cite{scarselli2009graph and have been successfully applied in diverse domains, including molecular chemistry \cite{gilmer2017mpnn , transport planning \cite{jiang2022graph , social networks \cite{fan2019graph , drug discovery \cite{xiong2021graph , and climate modeling \cite{lam2023graphcast .}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-198: However, strictly edge-based MPNNs neglect global relationships embedded in the graph topology.
elements.sent-2411-199: This locality bias introduces bottlenecks \cite{topping2021understanding,di2023over that result in the loss of higher-order structural signals \cite{ai2024a2gcn and impose theoretical limits on the patterns they can detect \cite{xu2018powerful .}}}
elements.sent-2411-200: As a result, single-hop models fail to identify intricate topological substructures \cite{zhang2023rethinking .}
elements.sent-2411-201: For example, in social networks, association groups with intricate topology often remain undetected by edge-based MPNNs \cite{xiong2024graph .}

elements.sent-2411-202: To address the locality limitation, recent high-order attention methods assign weights to $k$-simplexes \cite{huang2024higher and $k$-neighborhoods \cite{zhang2024hongat , or sample meta-paths originating from each node \cite{yang2021spagan,yang2016revisiting .}}}
elements.sent-2411-203: However, though these approaches excel at sampling within their problem contexts, they invariably rely on greedy sampling from a distribution of possible higher-order relationships.
elements.sent-2411-204: Such strategies often redraw previously selected samples, limiting the effective exploration of their intended search space.
elements.sent-2411-205: Consequently, the model’s ability to uncover latent topological structures is diminished \cite{zhang2023rethinking .}
elements.sent-2411-206: This limitation is exacerbated by the fact that the state space of possible $k$-hop relationships grows exponentially with $k$, making exhaustive sampling infeasible.
elements.sent-2411-207: Under a fixed sampling budget, greediness tends to bias selection toward a narrow subset of $k$-hop feature-space modalities, further under-representing higher-order concepts.
elements.sent-2411-208: To improve the discovery of latent topological structures, we propose the Higher-Order Graph Attention (HoGA) module, designed to capture global $k$-hop relationships even under stringent sampling budgets.
elements.sent-2411-209: HoGA assigns weights to higher-order connections by sampling fixed substructures, enriching the representation of global dependencies within the model’s latent reasoning.
elements.sent-2411-210: Specifically, HoGA constructs subgraphs of the $k$-hop neighborhood through a heuristically guided walk (Figure~\ref{fig:front_fig provides example walks).}
elements.sent-2411-211: HoGA's walk balances global and local diversity metrics, enabling high-fidelity approximation of the otherwise intractable $k$-hop state space via iterative sampling.
elements.sent-2411-212: Unlike conventional approaches, HoGA avoids revisiting previously explored $k$-hop features, thereby collecting distinct modalities in the underlying graph topology and enhancing their diversity.
elements.sent-2411-213: This diversity-driven heuristic increases coverage of the $k$-hop feature space, improving the model’s ability to detect association groups and other intricate topological substructures.
elements.sent-2411-214: We empirically evaluate the integration of the Higher-Order Graph Attention (HoGA) module into two distinct attention-based models, GAT \cite{velickovic2018graph and GRAND \cite{chamberlain2021grand , across both homophilic and heterophilic benchmark node classification datasets \cite{yang2016revisiting .}}}
elements.sent-2411-215: Our results show that HoGA-enhanced models (HoGA-GAT and HoGA-GRAND) achieve substantial accuracy gains over their respective base models.
elements.sent-2411-216: Relative to other baselines, they achieve statistically significant gains on six out of eight datasets evaluated on.
elements.sent-2411-217: By promoting sampling diversity, HoGA enables high-fidelity estimation of the $k$-hop feature space.
elements.sent-2411-218: We show through rigorous experimentation that applying HoGA to existing single-hop attention models, namely GAT \cite{velickovic2018graph and GRAND \cite{chamberlain2021grand , yields significant accuracy improvements over both homophilic and heterophilic node classification benchmarks datasets.}}

elements.sent-2411-219: Message-passing regimes that emphasize strict locality suffer from several well-known issues, including oversmoothing, oversquashing, and provably limited expressive power~\cite{morris2019weisfeiler .}
elements.sent-2411-220: Since the update function in MPNNs is generally not injective, the expressive power of first-order message passing is inherently limited.
elements.sent-2411-221: The set of non-isomorphic graphs that any $1$-hop aggregation scheme can uniquely color is a strict subset of those distinguishable by the $1$-WL test \cite{morris2019weisfeiler .}
elements.sent-2411-222: Enhancing MPNN expressivity beyond the $1$-WL test has therefore become a critical research area.
elements.sent-2411-223: Due to the finite capacity of hidden representations, long-range information is gradually lost through message passing.
elements.sent-2411-224: Analytically, oversquashing can be quantified in terms of information bottleneck severity \cite{di2023over,topping2021understanding .}
elements.sent-2411-225: To mitigate this, graph rewiring techniques either add \cite{gutteridge2023drew,arnaiz2022diffwire or remove \cite{karhadkar2022fosr edges to improve information flow.}}
elements.sent-2411-226: However, such rewiring does not explicitly preserve the original topology and may create superficial connections while removing genuine ones.
elements.sent-2411-227: In contrast, $k$-hop aggregation maintains topology while reducing the effective commute time between any two nodes by a factor of $k$, thereby theoretically alleviating oversquashing \cite{topping2021understanding .}
elements.sent-2411-228: Overlapping receptive fields cause node feature vectors to converge toward constant values, corresponding to the Dirichlet energy approaching zero \cite{rusch2023survey .}
elements.sent-2411-229: In contrast, feature diversity between nodes tends to increase with hop distance within the $k$-hop neighborhood \cite{ai2024a2gcn .}
elements.sent-2411-230: Single-hop attention schemes are well studied \cite{kipf2016semi,xu2018powerful,gilmer2017mpnn,chamberlain2021grand .}
elements.sent-2411-231: Notably, \citet{velickovic2018graph introduced self-attention on graph edges.}
elements.sent-2411-232: Recent work has extended attention to paths of arbitrary length, treating edges as paths of length one \cite{zhang2024hongat,huang2024higher .}
elements.sent-2411-233: However, the non-polynomial growth of the $k$-hop state space remains a major challenge when aggregating higher-order feature vectors.
elements.sent-2411-234: Several approaches aim to leverage long-range relationships while maintaining tractability.
elements.sent-2411-235: For example, Abboud et al.~\cite{abboud2022shortest map the entire $k$-hop neighborhood into a single feature vector via an injective aggregation function.}
elements.sent-2411-236: Yet, due to the inherent diversity of $k$-hop features, such aggregation risks oversmoothing distinct modalities.
elements.sent-2411-237: Walk-based approaches \cite{yeh2023random,kong2022geodesic,li2020distance,michel2023path sample the $k$-hop neighborhood by constructing length-$k$ paths.}
elements.sent-2411-238: For instance, Michel et al.\cite{michel2023path aggregate samples from the set of shortest and simple paths between node pairs into a single feature vector, while Kong et al.\cite{kong2022geodesic pool features from nodes along shortest paths by concatenating degree embeddings.}}
elements.sent-2411-239: These methods offer a flexible multi-hop paradigm, reducing a priori topological constraints while avoiding exponential state-space growth.
elements.sent-2411-240: Despite these advances, little attention has been given to methods that directly operate on the complete set of $k$-hop neighbors—despite their theoretical advantages for uncovering richer topological substructures \cite{zhang2023rethinking .}
elements.sent-2411-241: To address this gap, we propose a graph attention module that iteratively samples from the global $k$-hop feature space.
elements.sent-2411-242: Our experiments demonstrate significant performance improvements over state-of-the-art higher-order attention models.
elements.sent-2411-243: A key component of GNNs is their ability to directly learn fixed ground truth topology over a non-Euclidean domain.
elements.sent-2411-244: Here, we formulate the notion of local message-passing operators on graphs and the node classification task.
elements.sent-2411-245: Intuitively, $i, j \in V$ are variables from a state space over a real-world domain, {\it e.g. , individuals or papers within social or citation networks.}
elements.sent-2411-246: The topology described within $E$ defines the inherent relationships between these variables: if $(i, j) \in E$, $i$, and $j$ are directly related.
elements.sent-2411-247: Alternatively, should a path $\mathcal{P = (i_1, \dots, i_k)$ of length $k$ exist between $i_1=i$ and $i_k=j$, we denote $i$ and $j$ as having a relation by casualty of the nodes in the path $i_1, \dots, i_k \in V$.}
elements.sent-2411-248: Furthermore, owing to the sequential dependence on $k$ variables, the relationship between $\mathbf{x _{i_k}$ and $\mathbf{x _{i_1}$ is of order $k$.}}
elements.sent-2411-249: Correspondingly, each variable $i \in V$ is associated with a {\it feature-vector $\mathbf{x _i(t)$ at depth $t$.}}
elements.sent-2411-250: MPNNs are information aggregation schemes that concurrently aggregate nodal features across the whole graph.
elements.sent-2411-251: Notably, initial schemes, such as those used in \citet{kipf2016semi,xu2018powerful,hamilton2017inductive and \citet{velickovic2018graph , focused on aggregation of node $i$'s direct neighbors at a shortest path distance of one from $i$.}}

elements.sent-2411-256: To train our network, we use the Cross Entropy Loss function, which for output logits $\hat{y _i$ is given as: \begin{equation}\mathcal{L}_{}(y, \hat{y}) = - \sum_{l=1}^{\left\vert S \right\vert} y_l \log(\hat{y}_l).\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-257: In the single-hop setting, attention weights $\alpha_{i,j}$ were strictly considered to be along edges $(i, j) \in E$ \cite{velickovic2018graph .}
elements.sent-2411-258: The corresponding attention matrix $\mathbf{A (\mathbf{x (t))$ is therefore only non-zero on edges of the graph: \begin{equation}\label{eq:attn} \mathbf{A}(\mathbf{x}(t))_{i,j} = \begin{cases} \displaystyle \alpha_{i, j} \text{if } (i, j) \in E, \displaystyle 0 \text{otherwise}. \end{cases}\end{equation} Here, $\alpha_{i,j}$ is a normalised attention coefficient calculated by learning the parameters $\theta$ of the neural network $a_\theta(\cdot, \cdot)$: \begin{equation}\alpha_{i,j} = \frac{\exp \left( a_\theta(\mathbf{x}_i(t), \mathbf{x}_j(t)) \right)}{\sum_{l \in \mathcal{N}_1(i) \cup \{ i \}} \exp \left( a_\theta(\mathbf{x}_i(t), \mathbf{x}_l(t)) \right)}.\end{equation}.}}

elements.sent-2411-264: By capturing these relationships, our HoGA module improves the ability of MPNNs to recognize complex structural patterns.

elements.sent-2411-283: We formulate a simple walk-based method that aims to select $(i, j) \in E_k$, where the expected discrepancy between $\mathbf{x _i = \mathbf{x _i(0)$ and $\mathbf{x _j = \mathbf{x _j(0)$ is maximal.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-284: Given a history buffer $H = \{\!\{ \mathbf{x _1, \dots, \mathbf{x _n \}\!\}$ of size $n$ and a current node of interest $i \in V$, we select a node $j$ from candidate set $\mathcal{N _k(i)$ with probability $p \sim s_n$, where $s_n$ is the dissimilarity score between feature vectors: \begin{equation}s_n = \gamma \cdot f(\mathbf{x}_i, \mathbf{x}_j) + (1 - \gamma) \cdot f(\hat{\mathbf{x}}, \mathbf{x}_j), \label{eq:dissim}\end{equation} \begin{equation}f(\mathbf{x}_i, \mathbf{x}_j) = 1 - \frac{\mathbf{x}_i \cdot \mathbf{x}_j}{\|\mathbf{x}_i\| \cdot \|\mathbf{x}_j\|},\end{equation} \begin{equation}\hat{\mathbf{x}} = \sum_{1 \leq t \leq n} \gamma^{n-t} \cdot \mathbf{x}_{i_t}. \label{eq:buffer}\end{equation} The cosine dissimilarity $f(\cdot, \cdot)$ measures the discrepancy between $\mathbf{x _i$ and $\mathbf{x _j$ in terms of their collinearity.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-285: The parameter $\gamma \in [0, 1]$ is the decay rate, and $\hat{\mathbf{x }$ represents an exponential moving average over $H$.}

elements.sent-2411-287: The term $\gamma$ acts as the decay rate in Equation~\ref{eq:buffer , and balances the contributions between the greedy and history buffer steps.}

elements.sent-2411-291: The following theorem and corollary formalize how HoGA reduces the likelihood of repetitive sampling by avoiding re-entry into cycles.
elements.sent-2411-292: The probability that a walk traverses the cycle exactly in order is: \begin{equation}\mathbb{P}(i_n = j_1, \dots, i_{n+L} = j_L \mid C) = \prod_{1 \leq l \leq L} \frac{\omega_{j_l, j_{l+1}, n+l}}{\sum_{m \in \mathcal{N}_k(j_l)} \omega_{j_l, m, n+l}}.\end{equation}.
elements.formula-2411-016: \begin{equation}P(i_n = j_1, \dots, i_{n+L} = j_L \mid C) = \prod_{1 \leq l \leq L} \frac{\omega_{j_l, j_{l+1}, n+l}}{\sum_{m \in N_k(j_l)} \omega_{j_l, m, n+l}}.\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-293: The edge weights $\omega_{i,q,\tau}$ are updated each walk iteration by the non-greedy component of the walk, and are given by: \begin{equation}\omega_{i, q, \tau} = \gamma \left(f(x_i, x_q) - \delta_q(H_\tau)\right) + \delta_q(H_\tau),\end{equation} with $\delta_q(H_\tau)$ denoting the history buffer term at time $\tau$, and $f$ a dissimilarity function.
elements.sent-2411-294: Then, the probability of avoiding the cycle is proportional to: \begin{equation}\frac{\delta_q(H_\tau)}{\sum_{m \in \mathcal{N}_k(i_{n+l-1})} \delta_m(H_\tau)},\end{equation} where $\delta_q(H_\tau)$ denotes the history buffer constraint at time $\tau$.
elements.sent-2411-295: In contrast, a greedy walk re-enters cycles with constant probability, repeatedly sampling from the union of all cycles in the subgraph.
elements.sent-2411-296: Our corollary shows that, by leveraging a history buffer, the walk avoids previously visited topological substructures.
elements.sent-2411-297: Specifically, the history buffer constraint $\delta_q(H_\tau)$ acts as a repulsion mechanism, accelerating convergence of the empirical distribution to the true underlying distribution over $G$.
elements.sent-2411-298: Its effect is inversely proportional to the multiplicity of $q \in H_\tau$.

elements.sent-2411-318: We conduct empirical studies to address the following question: Is the application of higher-order attention via direct sampling of the $k$-hop neighborhood a viable approach for multi-hop aggregation? Our analysis is broken up into three components:.
elements.sent-2411-319: To what extent does the HoGA model mitigate the oversmoothing effect, and is the model's accuracy stable across different choices of maximum nodal distance?.
elements.sent-2411-320: What is the effectiveness of the heuristic walk sampling method compared to simpler sampling methods that do not consider global graph structure?.
elements.sent-2411-321: Specifically, we demonstrate the efficacy of our method through comparisons with state-of-the-art higher-order attention methods \cite{huang2024higher,abu2019mixhop,yang2021spagan , and Fourier methods \cite{he2021bernnet,kipf2016semi,defferrard2016convolutional .}}
elements.sent-2411-322: We evaluate all models on core benchmark node classification datasets, wherein the dataset comprises a single graph: Cora, Citeseer, and Pubmed \cite{yang2016revisiting .}
elements.sent-2411-323: We also evaluate other diverse datasets of variable size: Amazon Computers, Amazon Photos, and CoAuthor Computer Science \cite{shchur2018pitfalls .}
elements.sent-2411-324: In addition to these homophilic datasets, we also evaluate on the heterophilic datasets, Wisconsin and Texas.. {\bf Baseline Models.}
elements.sent-2411-325: We evaluate models that incorporate our higher-order attention module and compare them with other non-local aggregation schemes, which utilize either meta-paths, SPAGAN \cite{yang2021spagan , and topological structure, HiGCN \cite{huang2024higher .}}
elements.sent-2411-326: We also evaluate against STAGNN \cite{huang2023tailoring , a multi-hop transformer model, and DIFFformer \cite{wu2023difformer , a diffusion-based architecture.}}
elements.sent-2411-327: Furthermore, our study includes single-hop spectral methods such as APPNP \cite{gutteridge2023drew , BernNet \cite{he2021bernnet , and GCN \cite{kipf2016semi .}}}
elements.sent-2411-328: Additionally, we compare our higher-order attention models with their single-hop counterparts; GAT \cite{velickovic2018graph and GRAND \cite{chamberlain2021grand .}}
elements.sent-2411-329: We use the original parameter configurations for all baselines.
elements.sent-2411-330: We cover in this section various settings used throughout our empirical evaluations.
elements.sent-2411-331: The source code, along with data splits and samples of the $k$-hop neighborhood for all experiments, is provided in the supplementary materials.
elements.sent-2411-332: On Cora, Citeseer, and Pubmed, we use the public train, test, and validation set splits proposed in the original paper \cite{yang2016revisiting .}
elements.sent-2411-333: On the remaining datasets, we split the graph by randomly selecting nodes, where each set comprises 60\%, 20\%, and 20\% of all nodes.
elements.sent-2411-334: We set the random jump probability to 5\% when running our heuristic walk algorithm, and limit the maximum number of edges obtained from any sampling procedure to $90,000$ to reduce runtime.
elements.sent-2411-335: We use eight higher-order attention heads in the first network layer, and one in the subsequent layers.
elements.sent-2411-336: To avoid excessive fine-tuning, we set the maximum $k$-hop value to $K=3$ for all graphs, and keep the $k$-hop samples consistent across all layers.
elements.sent-2411-337: To reduce the variability in model performance due to random seed, we repeat all experiments $20$ times, re-initializing our models with a new seed at each repetition.
elements.sent-2411-338: To train and evaluate our models, we run our experiments using an A100 GPU.
elements.sent-2411-339: We evaluate the significance of the empirical model performance by using the Wilcoxon signed rank test with a confidence threshold of 5\% \definecolor{darkgreen {rgb {0.0, 0.5, 0.0 \definecolor{darkblue {rgb {0.0, 0.0, 0.5.}}}}}}
